title: Today's Tech Brief: Cisco sings Mythos' praises - but doesn't say how many bugs the model uncovered
summary: Cisco praises AI bug hunting model, Microsoft introduces AI personal assistant, and Anthropic expands Project Glasswing
imageAlt: Image from source article for Cisco sings Mythos' praises - but doesn't say how many bugs the model uncovered
imageCaption: Cisco sings Mythos' praises - but doesn't say how many bugs the model uncovered
imageSourceTitle: Cisco sings Mythos' praises - but doesn't say how many bugs the model uncovered

body.text: I've got a few tech updates for you today. First off, Cisco is raving about an AI bug hunting model from Mythos. According to a report from The Register, Cisco is thrilled with the results, but is being pretty coy about just how many bugs the model uncovered. It's a bit like saying your new diet is working, but not revealing the number on the scale. I'm curious to see if they'll share more info down the line.

The idea here is that AI can help sniff out bugs in code more efficiently than humans. It's an interesting approach, especially for companies like Cisco that rely on complex software. I'm wondering if this could be a game-changer for the industry, or if it's just a nice-to-have tool. Either way, it's cool to see AI being used in new and creative ways.

Next up, Microsoft has introduced a new AI personal assistant called Scout. Built on OpenClaw, Scout aims to be Microsoft's 'first real personal assistant.' I'm intrigued to see how this competes with existing virtual assistants and whether it can truly make a dent in the market. With all the AI assistants out there, it's getting crowded - but maybe that's a good thing for consumers.

Cisco sings Mythos' praises - but doesn't say how many bugs the model uncovered is also worth a quick mention. Microsoft Scout is a new AI personal assistant built on OpenClaw, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
